A study<sup>12</sup> of the effects of irradiation on the tensile properties of INOR-8 at high temperature showed that the stress-strain relationship was not affected by irradiation but that the high-temperature ductility was reduced.
Ductility was also observed to decrease as the strain rate was reduced from 0.2 to 0.002 in./min.
In this study, INOR-8 creep specimens from heats used in the MSRE will be irradiated at approximately $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to dose levels ranging from $10^{19}$ to $2 \times 10^{21}$ nvt.
Postirradiation creep tests will be run at 1100, 1300, and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for times ranging to 1000 hr.
A creep test experiment (ORR-135) was designed and built for irradiation in the ORR poolside facility, P-5.
It contains five tensile creep bars made from heat 5081, INOR-8.
INOR-8 specimens were made from heats 5081 and 5065 in the shape of miniature tensile bars approximately 2 in. long and with a gage length 1.0 in. long and 0.125 in. in diameter.
The tensile bars were welded end to end to form two rods approximately 64 in. long.
In the final assembly, the graphite specimens will be arranged into a long subassembly having a rectangular cross section of $0.47 \times 0.66$ in.
4.12, and these will be surrounded by six INOR-8 specimen rods and three INOR-8 tubes containing flux monitor wires.
The entire assembly will be held in a 0.200-in.-diam perforated tube.
After six months of reactor operation, two INOR-8 specimens, a flux monitor, and one graphite subassembly will be removed from the reactor and examined, and new specimens will replace those removed.

The analysis of the INOR-8 specimens will include (1) metallographic examination for structural changes, corrosion effects, and possible layer formations; (2) tensile properties and creep properties, with emphasis on creep ductility; (3) a general check for material integrity and dimensional changes; and (4) chemical analysis for composition changes and fission product deposition.

The graphite specimen analysis will include (1) metallographic examination for structural changes and material deposition; (2) radiographic examination for salt permeation and possible wetting effects; (3) flexural strength tests; (4) dimensional checks for shrinkage effects; (5) chemical analysis for salt and fission product deposition; (6) a general inspection for integrity; and (7) possibly physical properties such as electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and Hall coefficient.

The last report<sup>1</sup> reviewed the more significant features of irradiation experiments MTR-47-1 through MTR-47-5 on the compatibility and stability of MSRE materials in a fissioning environment and briefly described preliminary results from experiment MTR-47-6.
The chemical, radiographic, and metallographic examinations of the 47-5 specimens have since been completed, and only a few of the scheduled 47-6 postirradiation examinations remain to be completed.

In the early irradiations of the MTR-47 series, the INOR-8-fuel-graphite test assemblies generally survived irradiation without gross damage to the test materials, but evidence was observed of slow fluorine gas evolution from the irradiated fuel that occurred after solidification and cooling to room temperature.

The 47-5 test, in which two of the INOR-8 capsules were equipped with gas lines to sample the cover gas during irradiation, showed<sup>1</sup> that fuel radiolysis occurred only during reactor shutdowns when the fuel solidified and cooled to $35^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Thin deposits of uranium, up to $4\mathrm{mg}$ per square centimeter of exposed graphite, were observed on submerged graphite specimens.
The 47-3 molybdenum specimens were badly corroded.

The total weight of other fuel salt cations per gram of capsule 3 graphite was $12\mathrm{mg}$ .

The graphite from capsule 1 (graphite crucible) contained only 2 to $4\mathrm{mg}$ of uranium, along with $0.2\mathrm{mg}$ of other fuel cations and $0.7\mathrm{mg}$ of iron, molybdenum, and nickel (combined) per gram of sample.
Qualitative gamma scans of the graphite specimens (without radiochemical separations) generally revealed much higher $^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ concentrations than in irradiated fuel samples (see below).

The agreement of the chemical analyses for bulk constituents was disappointing, with more than half the analyses varying from the original cation concentrations by more than $10\%$ .
The individual analytical methods had a tested accuracy of 3 to $5\%$ .
Also, the sum of the cation percentages for each sample was invariably lower, in several cases by 5 wt $\%$ out of a total of 33 wt $\%$ , than for the original salt.
The temperature of the capsule F graphite should have been high since it contained the 4 wt $\%$ $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ fuel and was positioned at the high-flux end of the in-pile assembly.

The gamma spectra of the 47-5 salt samples appeared different from those of previous runs because the 6- to 9-month delay between irradiation and observation enhanced the 30-year $^{137}\mathrm{Cs}$ and the 1-year $^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ peaks relative to those of shorter-lived fission products.

The predominant peaks in bulk salt samples were $^{144}\mathrm{Ce},$ $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}-^{95}\mathrm{Nb},$ $^{137}\mathrm{Cs},$ and $^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ with relative intensities (dis/min) of approximately 100, 40, 30, and 1

The $^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ showed the most variation, from 0.1 to 90 on the above relative basis ( $^{144}\mathrm{Ce} = 100$ ), reaching the higher values for samples taken near the top surface of the fuel salt.

(Later experience has shown that much of the ruthenium activity may be lost in the usual salt dissolution by boiling sulfuric-nitric-boric acid without a condenser.) The drop-lets of salt from capsule F showed a $^{137}\mathrm{Cs}$ activity four times the $^{144}\mathrm{Ce}$ activity, possibly indicating volatilization of CsF from the fuel-impregnated core of this high-flux capsule.

The $^{144}\mathrm{Ce}$ to $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}-^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ ratios for most salt samples varied little from the average value of 2.5; a few samples of material adhering to the INOR-8 wall in the vapor phase contained less $^{144}\mathrm{Ce}$ and more $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}-^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ activity.
INOR-8 corrosion products were determined in the middle portions of fuel salt from the four large capsules.
The concentration of iron varied from 40 to 230 ppm, molybdenum from 20 to 120 ppm, and nickel from less than 70 to 160 ppm.
Chromium and manganese were not detected to limits of 4 and 2 ppm respectively.
The corrosion product levels were highest in capsule 3 salt.

The results of the detailed examinations of the graphite, INOR-8, and fuel salt specimens from the six irradiated capsules of experiment MTR-47-5 confirmed the previously suggested relation between material incompatibilities and fuel radiolysis at low temperature.
The INOR-8 withstood exposure to fluorine and reduced fuel, as well as extended contact with fissioning fuel, with only slight indications of corrosive attack.
The principal innovation of the 47-6 design was the provision of individual heaters on the four capsules to keep the fuel salt molten during reactor shutdowns.
Four of these assemblies were mounted vertically in a horizontal array in the nose cone and were numbered 1 through 4 in order of increasing flux position.
Each capsule heater consisted of a spiral of 1/8-in.-OD Inconel-sheathed, MgO-insulated heater wire embedded in a cylindrical solid copper body by a flame spraying technique.
The power rating of each heater assembly was 8000 w at 110 v ac.
The heaters operated automatically when the reactor shut down to maintain the fuel salt molten during the complete 12-week irradiation period.

As in previous experiments of this series, the nose end of the in-pile assembly could be mechanically moved 13 in. toward or away from the MTR core during irradiation, permitting controlled variation of the capsule fission power density.
Capsules 1, 2, and 4 each contained a 1.35-in.-long, 1/2-in.-diam graphite core held in position by the thermocouple and a bottom pin.
The core of capsule 3 was only half as long, to test the effect of exposed graphite area on $\mathrm{CF_4}$ evolution.
$27\mathrm{g}$ ( $12\mathrm{cm}^3$ at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) of fuel salt, leaving a capsule gas space of about $4\mathrm{cm}^3$ .

Capsule 1 fuel salt contained $4.0\mathrm{mole}\% \mathrm{UF}_4$ , that in capsules 2 and 3 contained $0.9\mathrm{mole}\% \mathrm{UF}_4$ (like MSRE fuel), and the fuel in capsule 4 contained $0.5\mathrm{mole}\% \mathrm{UF}_4$ , the uranium being highly enriched ( $93.26\%$ ) in each case.
The sealed capsules 1 and 4 each contained three molybdenum corrosion specimens, 1 in. $\times 1/8$ in. $\times 0.020$ in., mounted vertically in slots in the capsule side and bottom walls.
Capsules 2 and 3 were equipped with gas lines for purging the capsule gas spaces with purified helium and for sampling the capsule effluents.
The first 7 to 9 in. of the capsule inlet and outlet lines was 0.305 in.
The remaining 50 ft of each line to the sample station was 1/8-in.-OD stainless steel tubing, shielded by 6 in. of lead between the reactor plug and the sample station.
The supply helium passed through a Grove pressure regulator to one of a set of three capillary flowmeters calibrated to measure flows between 50 and $5000~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{hr}$ .

Just prior to entering the sample station adjacent to the reactor beam hole, the helium was purified by a Linde 13X molecular sieve drying column and a titanium sponge trap maintained at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A simplified schematic drawing of the flow circuit for the capsule 3 part of the sample station is shown in Fig.
The sample station cubicle, shielded by at least 6 in. of lead, enclosed a similar circuit for capsule 2.

After collecting all the other gases from the helium for the desired time (1 to $48\mathrm{hr}$ ), the helium was evacuated from the trap, the trap was warmed to $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the released gases were swept with helium into an evacuated sample bottle, furnishing a $24.6\text{-cm}^3$ gas sample at 600 mm pressure and room temperature.
The concentration effected by this collection method was more than a factor of 100 for the usual sampling times and purge flows.

Concentrated gas samples were taken over a wide range of the experimental variables, representing capsule temperatures from 800 to $1440^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , purge-gas flow rates from 50 to $5000~\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{hr}$ , and fission power densities from 0 to about $70~\mathrm{w / cm}^3$ .

Qualitative information on the rate of radiolysis of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ was obtained from the regular concentrated gas samples taken at different purge flow rates, but more definitive measurements were obtained by passing helium- $\mathrm{CF_4}$ mixtures through capsule 3 and determining the change in thermal conductivity with a sensitivity of $0.005\% \mathrm{CF_4}$ .
Gas mixtures containing $0.1\%$ $\mathrm{CF_4}$ and $5\% \mathrm{CF_4}$ were passed through the capsule 3 flow circuit for these tests.

A gas chromatographic method for detecting parts per billion levels of impurities in helium, using a newly developed sensor which measured impurity-caused change in helium breakdown voltage, was applied to the detection of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ in direct unconcentrated samples of capsule 3 purge gas.

A third special purge-gas mixture, containing $1\mathrm{ppm}$ $\mathrm{CF_4}$ , was passed through the capsule 3 circuit to calibrate the detector and to measure the change when the $1\mathrm{-ppm}$ mixture was passed through capsule 3.

When the four MTR cycles of irradiation, accumulating 1500 hr at full reactor power, had been completed, the capsules were allowed to decay for 32 hr with the fuel salt molten, in the hope of minimizing fuel radiolysis
The fuel from the last capsule opened was isolated from the other capsule components within 48 hr of the freezing of the salt.
An instrument power failure for 20 min during a reactor shutdown period permitted a momentary freezing of the fuel salt and cooling to $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The 48-hr delay in separating the fuel salt from the other capsule components after the final cooling of the in-pile assembly was longer than planned, but subsequent examinations indicated that no serious damage was done.

No $\mathrm{CF_4}$ or other volatile fluoride was detected by mass analysis in any of the regular concentrated gas samples (detection limit 2 to $10~\mathrm{ppm}$ ).
The gas chromatograph analyses on direct unconcentrated samples of capsule 3 effluent likewise indicated no $\mathrm{CF_4}$ , with a detection limit of about $0.2~\mathrm{ppm}$ .
The most sensitive determination involved a 48-hr sample collection from capsule 3 operating at a power density of $68~\mathrm{w/cm^3}$ .
The sample analysis showed less than $2~\mathrm{ppm}~\mathrm{CF_4}$ and over $2000~\mathrm{ppm}$ of fission product xenon.
This demonstration accomplished one of the major objectives of the 47-6 experiment.
The total xenon and krypton analyses showed a wide scatter and a low bias by a factor of about 2, leading to a suspicion that these fission products were released in occasional bursts.
Xenon isotopic analyses revealed low $^{131}\mathrm{Xe}$ and $^{132}\mathrm{Xe}$ percentages compared to fission yields.
These particular isotopes had relatively long-lived precursors, 8-day $^{131}\mathrm{I}$ and 77-hr $^{132}\mathrm{Te}$ .
It is thought that the volatilized precursors were swept out of the system during purging, resulting in low quotas of $^{131}\mathrm{Xe}$ and $^{132}\mathrm{Xe}$ in subsequent gas samples.

It was also noted that the $^{136}\mathrm{Xe}/^{134}\mathrm{Xe}$ ratios were intermediate between those calculated from fission yields for no conversion of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ to $^{136}\mathrm{Xe}$ by neutron capture and for conversion corresponding to the exposure of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ to the neutron flux for its complete 13.3-hr mean life.
From these data it was calculated that the average residence time of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the high-flux region was about 7 hr.
The gamma spectrometer results on the more radio-active gas samples showed $^{133}\mathrm{Xe}$ as the only detectable activity.
The detection limit for $^{131}\mathrm{I}$ and $^{129}\mathrm{Te}$ activities was $0.05\%$ of the $^{133}\mathrm{Xe}$ activity.

Since no $\mathrm{CF_4}$ was detected in capsule 3 effluent at any purge flow rates, the regular concentrated gas samples yielded no quantitative information on the rate of radiolysis of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ .
During the third MTR cycle of irradiation, special radiolysis tests were performed, passing helium containing $0.1\%$ $\mathrm{CF_4}$ or $5\%$ $\mathrm{CF_4}$ through capsule 3.
With the instrument set at highest sensitivity, $1\text{-mv}$ output change (read on a $10\text{-mv}$ recorder) corresponded to $0.05\%$ $\mathrm{CF_4}$ concentration change.
The first dynamic tests were made with helium containing $0.1\% \mathrm{CF}_4$ until it was determined that the radiolysis rate was too low to be measured with any accuracy.

A static test, isolating $0.1\% \mathrm{CF}_4$ in the capsule for 10 hr, indicated a radiolysis rate of $4.2\% / \mathrm{hr}$ , with capsule temperature at $1077^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ , minimum insertion (power density of approximately $29 \mathrm{~w/cm}^3$ ), and a flushing rate of $192 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{hr}$ .
The $5\%$ $\mathrm{CF_4}$ mixture was then flushed through the system, and radiolysis runs gave the results shown in Table 5.3.

The tabulated data indicate a $\mathrm{CF_4}$ radiolysis of less than $4\% / \mathrm{hr}$ above fuel fissioning at the design power density and temperature for the MSRE ( $14~\mathrm{w/cm^3}$ and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ).

As mentioned above, the low $^{131}\mathrm{Xe}$ and $^{132}\mathrm{Xe}$ percentages found in the isotopic analyses of the gas samples are consistent with volatilization of tellurium and iodine from the fuel salt.

Qualitative gamma scans of the graphite specimens (and their solutions) indicated higher $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ than $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}-^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ activities, a reversal of the situation in gamma scans of bulk salt samples.
The salt specimens from the 47-6 test were generally gray-green in color rather than black as in previous tests.
The salt from capsule 1 had segregated into two clearly separated regions, one pale green and the other black.
The paler color of the 47-6 salt specimens may be related to the suppression of radiolysis during reactor shutdowns or to the heating for 32 hr after the final shutdown.
The $\mathrm{Li_2BeF_4}$ phase was identified in all samples and the $3\mathrm{LiF} \cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ phase in a few (notably in a black sample of capsule 1 salt).

The presence of the above-mentioned phases was confirmed, another uranium-containing phase, $7\mathrm{LiF} \cdot 6\mathrm{UF}_4$ , was observed, and very curiously no zirconium-containing phases were detected with certainty.
The absence of the latter and the presence of $3\mathrm{LiF} \cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ indicated that the cooling occurred under conditions quite different from equilibrium.

The types of sample examined included top, middle, and bottom samples of bulk fuel salt, volatilized beads, and adherent scale from the INOR-8 wall in the gas space, green and black salt from capsule 1, middle salt adjacent to graphite and to INOR-8, and salt from the top meniscus of the fuel.
The principal activities were $^{95}\mathrm{Zr - }^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ , $^{141}\mathrm{Ce}$ , $^{140}\mathrm{Ba - }^{140}\mathrm{La}$ , and $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ in these relatively short-cooled samples.

The principal activities in bulk fuel samples were $^{95}\mathrm{Zr} - ^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ and $^{141}\mathrm{Ce}$ , usually in that order, but with $^{141}\mathrm{Ce}$ often predominating in bottom salt samples.
In the salt bead samples from the purged capsules, $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ was the major activity, and it was prominent in the samples of scale on INOR-8 in the gas-phase region.
The middle salt adjacent to the capsule wall showed an $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ activity higher than that of $^{141}\mathrm{Ce}$ or $^{140}\mathrm{Ba} - ^{140}\mathrm{La}$ .

The gamma scan of an oxalate solution leach of the capsule 4 gas lines adjacent to capsule 4 showed high $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ and $^{95}\mathrm{Zr} - ^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ , low $^{140}\mathrm{Ba} - ^{140}\mathrm{La}$ , and no $^{141}\mathrm{Ce}$ .
Fuel meniscus samples generally showed higher $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ activities than bulk fuel samples.
The green salt from capsule 1 contained relatively more $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ than the black portion.
For chemical analyses, the fuel salt from each capsule was divided into a bulk salt sample ( $\sim 25\mathrm{g}$ ) and a bottom salt sample (1 to $2\mathrm{g}$ ).
From capsule 1, additional samples of segregated pale green and black salt were taken.

The molybdenum specimens from capsules 1 and 4, on visual low-magnification examination, showed no damage except for scratches probably made during disassembly of the capsules.
The specimens from capsule 4 were mottled in appearance, as if by the plating of a noble metal.
Gamma scans of specimens from both capsules showed the major activities were $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ and $^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ .
The only other activity noted was $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}-^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ .
The specimens broke cleanly after bending $5^{\circ}$ .

In the MTR-47-6 test four capsules containing INOR-8, MSRE fuel salt, and MSRE graphite were irradiated for 1500 hr at fuel power densities from 17 to $75\mathrm{w/cm^3}$ and temperatures up to $1440^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The mass analyses of the gas samples showed low and variable xenon and krypton yields, low helium impurity levels, and low $^{131}\mathrm{Xe}$ and $^{132}\mathrm{Xe}$ isotopic percentages.
The observed $^{136}\mathrm{Xe}/^{134}\mathrm{Xe}$ ratios suggest that the mean residence time of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the capsules was about 7 hr.

1 ppm of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ in helium when passed over molten fissioning fuel may have been due to the radiation-catalyzed reaction of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ with $\mathrm{H}_2$ or $\mathrm{CH_4}$ impurities in the helium.

The rate of $\mathrm{CF_4}$ radiolysis in contact with molten fissioning MSRE fuel was determined by thermal conductivity measurements and found to be less than $4\%$ per hour at MSRE power density and temperature.
The low rates observed preclude radiolysis effects on $\mathrm{CF_4}$ concentration in the 47-6 gas samples.
The graphite specimens from the 47-6 capsules were thoroughly analyzed for uranium content by a chemical fluorometric method and by delayed-neutron counting on activated samples.

There is evidence from the purification-plant inventory data that the composition of the salt delivered into the reactor system is of the design composition, $64.0 \pm 0.25$ mole $\%$ LiF and $36.0 \pm 0.25$ mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
A sample of the MSRE coolant, taken from one of the batches loaded into the reactor, showed a phase transition temperature of $457.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
This is within experimental error of $457.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the temperature of the peritectic reaction, which can occur only in compositions richer than 65.5 mole $\%$ LiF.
Chemical analyses of this material indicated its composition to be 63.63 mole $\%$ LiF-36.37 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
The thermal data indicate, however, that the material contained at least 65.5 mole $\%$ LiF, and thus confirm the composition indicated by the weights of the materials used in its preparation.

The average discrepancy between analytical values for LiF and $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ in $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ and $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ - $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ mixtures is $\sim -2.2$ mole $\%$ for LiF and $\sim +2.2$ mole $\%$ for $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ .

A new method, a modification of the hydrogen evolution method, is being tested for the determination of the reducing power. $^{38}$ The fuel sample is dissolved in tritiated $6\mathrm{M}$ hydrochloric acid to evolve tritium in proportion to the equivalents of reducing species in the sample.
The method is inherently quite sensitive; the limit of detection is 10 ppm for a 10-min count, but the sensitivity will probably be limited by the blank from radiolysis of the dissolver solution.
In each series tested, the precision of the method was less than $3\%$ .

The investigations in the MSRE coolant salt are, at the present time, concerned with a reduction wave which occurs in both the fuel solvent and coolant salts at about -1.2 v vs the platinum quasi-reference electrode.

To aid in the elucidation of the reductions occurring in this potential range, the controlled-potential polarograph ORNL model Q-1988 (adapted for fast scan) was modified to enable the use of the derivative circuitry.
The derivative current-voltage curves thus obtained indicate that the reduction at -1.2 v is even more complex than previously envisioned.
